body_txt: This is an extremely common practice in animation where, to establish a familial relationship, a child looks more or less identical to one of their parents, or they just look a lot like their family. This frequently occurs when a show uses garish or outlandish designs, where subtlety and fine physical detail are already lost, and we need a quick visual aid that other characters may not seem to notice. If a family is the stereotypical four-member " nuclear " one, the children will usually resemble the respective same-sex parents. In an attempt to mix things up, however, there may be a family in which the children resemble the respective opposite-sex parents instead. Another common variant is to have the children look nearly identical to the respective same-sex parents, but with the color schemes of the opposite-sex ones. If there is a Tomboy and Girly Girl or a Sensitive Guy and Manly Man , the tomboy or the manly man will usually resemble the father, while the girly girl or the sensitive guy will usually resemble the mother. Apart from its usage in character design, this trope can also play a role in the plot; often, someone notices how similar two characters look and thus figures out they are related to each other. A Super-Trope of Uncanny Family Resemblance (where the similarity is maintained further along the family tree), Hereditary Hairstyle , Family Eye Resemblance , Gender Equals Breed and Patchwork Kids (when the children have an obvious mix of their parents' physical traits). Compare Identical Grandson (where the similarities extend to ancestors or descendants who are several generations apart) and Generation Xerox (where not only the appearance but also the personality and experiences are repeated). Also compare and contrast Denial of Resemblance . See also Like Father, Like Son and Shared Family Quirks , when family members share similar behaviors and personality traits rather than physical traits. This might occur if an Actor Plays Their Own Relative , especially if that actor is also playing themselves . Contrast The Ugly Guy's Hot Daughter . Though sometimes exaggerated in fiction, this trope is Truth in Television , thanks to the mechanics behind our genetics and the way they're expressed.
